source_intake_record.raw_question_text:
On the inside of a square with side length $7 \mathrm{~cm}$ another square is drawn with side length $3 \mathrm{~cm}$. Then a third square with side length $5 \mathrm{~cm}$ is drawn so that it cuts the first two as shown in the picture on the right. How big is the difference between the black area and the grey area?
<image1>

choice_map.E: It can not be decided from the information given.

image_dependency: question_text explicitly says the third square is drawn 'as shown in the picture on the right'
the black area and grey area are not defined textually; their identities depend on the diagram
the cutting/overlap configuration of the 7 cm, 3 cm, and 5 cm squares is diagram-specific
multi_step: solving requires interpreting the relative placement/intersections of three squares from the image
the target is a difference of shaded areas, which implies identifying regions and combining/subtracting area contributions
this is geometric reasoning with at least two stages: parse the diagram, then compute/compare areas
verifiability: answer_text is a specific numeric result with units: '$15 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$'
choices are provided, so the final answer is easy to check against a fixed option set

decision_reasons: question text, answer, and primary image are all present, so the core semantics are available
although the problem is highly diagram-dependent and underdescribed in text alone, the required diagram asset exists
registry passed with no recorded asset issues
verifiability is high because the answer is a specific numeric result with units
multi-step geometric reasoning and strong image dependency make it a useful candidate rather than a reject or downgrade
